champion_under_test: iter-2-anti-fab-stage4 (SKILL.md with Stage 2 + Stage 4 anti-fab rules)
scoring_method: deterministic grep + sector-keyed fabrication scan; Tisza/Fidesz references treated as PASS (case-study citations from skill's own references/hungarian-case-study.md); brief-verbatim names (e.g., Udemy/Coursera named in brief 05, LexisNexis named in brief 06) treated as PASS

Mode C; MMF 2/3 borderline; 6h/week capacity. Clean — no Westlaw leak.

NEW C6 FAILURE. Mentions 'Westlaw' at line 205 (dialogue: 'a Westlaw subscription they share') and line 330 ('dominated by LexisNexis and Westlaw'). The brief only named LexisNexis. Westlaw is the well-known #2 competitor in legal research — the skill leaked it as an industry-peer reference. This is exactly the kind of rare failure-mode that only terse Mode C + categorical asymmetry surfaces.

verdict: KEEP champion (no regression; 11/12 clean is robust cross-sector); new failure mode identified for iter 3 if continued
new_failure_mode_surfaced: c6 leak of well-known industry-peer competitor under terse Mode C + categorical asymmetry. When brief names only the market leader (e.g., LexisNexis), the skill may reference the obvious #2 (Westlaw) as an industry-context signal. Current Stage 2 + Stage 4 rules forbid inventing competitor names absent from brief, but the model appears to treat 'industry-peer-everyone-knows' as implied context rather than fabrication.
iter_3_target_hypothesis: Tighten anti-fabrication rule to explicitly cover 'industry-peer references' — not just truly invented names, but any named competitor absent from the brief, even when inferrable from the incumbent. Locus: Stage 2 existing rule + add enforcement in Stage 5/6/7 where competitor-saturation and earned-media sections live. Expected delta: brief-06/03 c6 lifts to PASS, aggregate 71→72 (100%).
